title: Limitations of LLMs in Automotive Sales: What They Can't Commit To
summary: This document outlines ten key areas where Large Language Models (LLMs) fall short in the car buying process, emphasizing that while LLMs can explain concepts, they cannot handle binding financial commitments, real-time store realities, or build genuine customer relationships, which require human involvement and dealer systems.
text:
Short  version:  
 
LLMs
 
can
 
explain
 
car
 
buying.
 
They
 
shouldn’t
 
be
 
the
 
ones
 
to
 
commit
 
to
 
anything
 
that
 
involves
 
money,
 
risk,
 
or
 
accountability.
 
Here’s  what  they  won’t  really  give  customers  about  buying  a  car  (even  as  they  get  crazy  
good):
 
 
1.  A  binding  out-the-door  number  
An  LLM  can  estimate :  
●  Payments  
 ●  Taxes  &  fees  
 ●  “Rough”  out-the-door  prices  
 
But  it  can’t :  
●  Lock  a  VIN-specific,  contract-ready  OTD  price  
 ●  Guarantee  that  number  will  still  be  good  tomorrow  
 ●  Take  legal  responsibility  if  something  changes  
 
Anything  that  turns  into,  “Sign  here  and  we’re  on  the  hook,”  has  to  come  from  the  dealer’s  actual  
systems
 
and
 
people,
 
not
 
a
 
probabilistic
 
model.
 
 
2.  Real  underwriting  or  credit  decisions  
Even  if  it’s  integrated  with  tools,  the  LLM  itself  is  not  the  lender.  
It  can’t  truly:  
●  Pull  and  interpret  your  real  credit  file  on  its  own  authority  
 ●  Approve  or  deny  a  loan  
 
●  Promise  a  rate  or  term  the  bank  must  honor  
 
It  can  explain  how  credit  works.  It  cannot  be  the  one  taking  risk .  
 
3.  A  guaranteed  trade  value  
LLMs  can  talk  about:  
●  How  trade  values  are  calculated  
 ●  Typical  ranges  based  on  year/miles/condition  
 
They  can’t:  
●  See/smell/hear  the  car  
 ●  Catch  prior  accident  work  or  reconditioning  costs  
 ●  Actually  commit  the  store  to  a  guaranteed  “we’ll  cut  you  this  check”  number  
 
That  always  requires  a  human  +  physical  inspection  +  store  authority.  
 
4.  Physical  experience  and  gut  feel  
They  can’t  give  the  customer:  
●  What  it  feels  like  to  sit  in  the  seat  with  the  wheel  in  your  hands  
 ●  Whether  a  3rd-row  actually  works  for  their  kids  
 ●  Noise,  ride  quality,  “does  this  feel  like  me ?”  
 
LLMs  can  describe  those  things.  They  can’t  replace  a  test  drive  or  that  “this  is  the  one”  moment.  
 
5.  Real  accountability  if  things  go  sideways  
An  LLM:  
●  Can  apologize  
 ●  Can  “own”  a  mistake  in  text  
 
But  it  cannot:  
●  Pay  for  a  misquote  
 ●  Fix  a  blown  delivery  date  
 ●  Comp  you  a  service  visit  or  discount  to  make  something  right  
 
Customers  ultimately  need  a  person  and  an  organization  that  can  say,  
 
“Yep,
 
that’s
 
on
 
us.
 
Here’s
 
how
 
we’ll
 
fix
 
it.”
 
 
6.  Perfect,  real-time  store  reality  
Even  with  great  integrations,  there  are  hard  edges:  
●  Last-unit  inventory  (car  just  sold  5  minutes  ago)  
 ●  Micro-incentives  changing  this  morning  
 ●  Manager-specific  deals  (“I’ll  do  this  for  you  if  you  take  delivery  today”)  
 
LLMs  are  always  working  from  data  that’s  a  snapshot ,  not  the  actual,  messy,  political  reality  of  
a
 
store
 
on
 
a
 
Saturday
 
afternoon.
 
 
7.  True  advocacy  and  relationship  
They  can  simulate  empathy,  but  they  cannot:  
●  Actually  care  if  this  payment  stretches  your  budget  
 ●  Remember  your  kid’s  name  and  follow  up  in  three  years  because  they  want  to  
 
●  Build  a  relationship  that  feels  like,  “I’ve  got  a  person  at  that  store”  
 
For  most  people,  a  car  is  a  big,  emotional  purchase.  A  relationship  outlives  any  chat.  
 
8.  Power  inside  the  dealership  
LLMs  don’t  have:  
●  Authority  to  lean  on  service  for  a  faster  appointment  
 ●  Influence  with  the  GM  or  GSM  
 ●  The  ability  to  call  in  a  favor  or  say,  “Let  me  talk  to  my  manager,  I’ll  fight  for  this.”  
 
They  can  suggest  what  should  happen.  They  can’t  make  people  do  it.  
 
9.  Judgment  calls  on  edge  cases  
LLMs  struggle  (and  should  be  limited)  around:  
●  Weird  title  situations  (divorce,  estate,  out-of-state  issues)  
 ●  Messy  credit  stories  (“I  had  a  repo,  then  a  BK,  and  I  just  started  my  job…”)  
 ●  Ethical  lines  (“Should  I  roll  this  negative  equity  into  another  84-month  loan?”)  
 
You  don’t  want  a  model  making  moral  or  life  impact  calls.  That’s  human  territory.  
 
10.  The  feeling  of  safety  when  money  is  on  the  line  
They  can  reduce  confusion,  but  they  can’t  give:  
●  The  reassurance  of,  “You’re  all  set.  This  is  exactly  what’s  happening  next.”  
 ●  The  confidence  that  someone  who  knows  the  store,  the  lenders,  and  you  is  watching  
your
 
deal.
 
 
That  sense  of  “you’re  safe  in  this  process”  comes  from  transparent  systems  +  accountable  
humans
,
 
not
 
the
 
LLM
 
alone.
 
 
If  you  want  a  simple  line  you  can  use  with  dealers:  
“LLMs  are  for  clarity.  Humans  are  for  commitments  and  consequences.”  
That’s  the  gap  where  a  company  like  Strolid  lives:  
 
Let
 
the
 
LLM
 
handle
 
the
 
explaining,
 
searching,
 
and
 
prepping
 
—
 
then
 
bring
 
in
 
humans
 
(and
 
real
 
dealer
 
systems)
 
for
 
prices,
 
credit,
 
trades,
 
risk,
 
and
 
relationships.
